A Python workflow for obtaining historical data relating to immigration restrictions in the US

2024 marks the 100th anniversary of the creation of the discriminatory national origins quota system. The main goal of this project is to create a workflow that can serve as a resource for obtaining historical data relating to immigration restrictions in the US. The general methodology is using the Python requests library to make API calls from archives or libraries that have relevant historical collections. Then, using the Pandas library to create a dataset, clean the data and analyze it to provide examples of the kinds of insights it can provide. 

Tools used:
- Digital Public Library of America API
- `pandas` python library
- `requests` python library

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/bohagenpratt/1924_Project)

Analyzing sea level data across Puerto Rico from the NOAA Tides & Currents API

This project analyzes sea level data from the NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) Tides & Currents API for Puerto Rico, focusing on four coastal tide stations: San Juan (North), Parguera (South), Vieques Island (East), and Mayagüez (West). Using data from January 2015 to January 2025, the primary aim is to identify long-term trends in sea level rise and visualize the regional variations across the island's coasts. By investigating the data, the project seeks to uncover patterns in how natural disasters and environmental change have impacted sea levels over the past decade. These findings will help in understanding how Puerto Rico’s coastal areas may be at risk from ongoing environmental shifts.

The rationale behind this project is to investigate how sea levels have changed in Puerto Rico's coastal regions over time and to understand the broader implications of those changes. While sea level rise is a well-established global issue, the specific effects on Puerto Rico remain underexplored, especially in the context of natural disasters. By using historical data from NOAA's Tides & Currents API, this project aims to provide valuable insights into the temporal and spatial patterns of sea level rise, which could serve as a foundation for future research on environmental risks and community vulnerability. This analysis will provide data-driven insights that can contribute to the ongoing conversation about Puerto Rico’s environmental resilience.

Workflow:
- API request setup and call using `requests`
- Data cleaning and calculating averages using `pandas`
- Aggregating monthly and yearly averages
- Exporting aggregated results to CSV
- Visualizing the results using `plotly`

Tools used:
- `requests` python library
- `pandas` python library
- `plotly` python library
- NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) API

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/sofiarha/PR-SeaLevelRise)

A narrative of depictions of fatness from the MET's digital collections over time

This project explores the frequency and art mediums that use fatness in the MET's digital collections over time. It interrogates the historical shifts in the meaning of fatness as well as which narratives of fatness are overrepresented by cultural institutions like the Met.

Tools used:
- MET API
- `pandas` python library
- `requests` python library

AI Image Classification with Pulp Magazine Covers
Exploring how image classifiers trained on pornography would tag pulp magazines covers from the 1920s–1950s

This project explores how different AI vision models classify visual information using pulp magazine cover art from speculative fiction pulp magazines (mostly 1920s–1950s). The interactive website of this project allows users to view data visualizations of generated tags from image-classification or captioning models such as google/vit-base-patch16-224, SmilingWolf/wd-v1-4-vit-tagger-v2. By comparing these tags between models and to the actual cover art content, users can observe the models’ inaccuracies, biases, and limitations.

[View the live site](https://ink2819.github.io/Sci-Fi-Covers-I2T/)

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/ink2819/Sci-Fi-Covers-I2T)

Tools used:
- Internet Speculative Fiction Database API
- `transformers` python library
- `BeautifulSoup4` python libary
- `requests` python library

Automated poetry generation based on Shakespeare's sonnets

Shakespeare published 154 sonnets. This project seeks to automate the production of a poetic text constructed entirely from the first lines of Shakespeare's sonnets, totalling 11 new sonnets (154/14).

Tools used:
- Perplexity API
- `BeautifulSoup4` python library

A self-updating price tracker in the Runescape player marketplace

display them in a series of charts. It runs automatically every day at

workflow configured by Github Actions.

A Markov chain text generator trained from William S. Burroughs' Naked Lunch

Nova Bot is a Markov chain text generator seeded from the vocabulary
and cadence of William S. Burroughs' Naked Lunch. Enter a start word
and the app generates a stream of associative text, walking a
probabilistic path through the novel's language.

How It Works
- A vocabulary list (`hoard.txt`) filters which words are valid
- Word-to-word transitions are built from `naked_lunch.txt` at startup
- Given a start word, the app randomly walks the transition map to
  generate text
- A Flask web interface lets users enter a start word and generate
  output on demand

Tools used:
- `flask` python web framework
- `random` module in python

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/mstanisi/novabot)

A scraped dataset containing answers and authors and dates from the LA Times Crossword from 2019 to 2024

The dataset is scraped off the [LAX
crossword](https://laxcrossword.com/) blog that for over a decade has
posted the answers to the LA Times Crossword Puzzles. The dataset I
have created contains answers, authors, editors, and dates from nearly
every LA Times Crossword from 2019 to 2024. I have then used this
dataset to analyze thousands of crossword answers and identify the
patterns and trends that occur in this daily puzzle.

Admittedly, the original impetus for this project was because I kept
seeing the same answers in my crossword puzzles and I wanted to have
the data to back up and validate my indignation. Some have even gone
so far as to describe it as a "spite project", a baseless claim that I
refute. However, since work has begun, I think is interesting to see
the different trends that occur in these crossword puzzles that are a
daily part of peoples' lives, and see what aspects of popular culture,
current events, history, etc. have made it into these puzzles.

Workflow:
- Scrape the crossword clues and relevant metadata from the past 5
  years or so from the LAXCrossword blog using Python and
  BeautifulSoup and export it into a CSV using Pandas
- Clean the data into a usable dataset using a mixture of Pandas and
  Open Refine
- Analyze and visualize the dataset using tools like Pandas and the
  Voyant text analysis tool to search for trends and patterns
- Draw conclusions and prepare findings for presentation

Tools used:
- `pandas` python library
- `requests` python library
- `BeautifulSoup4` python library
- OpenRefine

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/aburke1999/LATimesCrosswordProject)

The dataset I have created contains answers, authors, editors, and dates from nearly every LA Times Crossword from 2019 to 2024. I have then used this dataset to analyze thousands of crossword answers and identify the patterns and trends that occur in this daily puzzle.

Admittedly, the original impetus for this project was because I kept seeing the same answers in my crossword puzzles and I wanted to have the data to back up and validate my indignation.   • Clean the data into a usable dataset using a mixture of Pandas and Open Refine

A visualization of destroyed Palestinian libraries alongside the lives lost on or around the same dates

Libraries are not just repositories of books—they are symbols of memory, culture, and resistance. In Palestine, the destruction of libraries is not an isolated act of war; it represents a broader erasure of heritage. This project visualizes the destruction of Palestinian libraries alongside the lives lost on or around the same dates, creating a powerful data-driven narrative that links cultural annihilation with human tragedy.

This project visualizes the destruction of Palestinian libraries
alongside the lives lost on or around the same dates, creating a
powerful data-driven narrative that links cultural annihilation with
human tragedy.


Workflow:

Data Extraction via Python & Pandas using a script has been developed
using Python and the Pandas library to scrape data from the Librarians
with Palestine 2024 Gaza Report.

This script has:
- Identified library names, destruction dates, and news sources.
- Normalized and exported the scraped information into a DataFrame and
  then CSV for analysis.
- Custom data set manual CSV entry of dates of loss of cultural
  institutions, martyr names, and casualty figures.

Tools used:
- `pandas` python library
- `requests` python library
- `BeautifulSoup4` python library
- `plotly` python library

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/christudo/mapping_libraries_site)

This project leverages publicly available data to analyze the causes of death across various U.S. cities

This project aims to analyze the causes of death across various U.S. cities by leveraging publicly available datasets accessed through open city data portals. Many municipalities now provide access to their data via APIs, often powered by platforms like Socrata, enabling efficient, programmatic retrieval of mortality-related statistics.

The research focuses on creating a comparative narrative instead of building a unified dataset by studying which causes of death different jurisdictions track and which remain unrecorded, or hidden, or inaccessible. Through utilizing open data APIs and data visualization tools, it will examine mortality patterns including overdose-related deaths, deaths in custody, and/or COVID-19 related deaths while exposing structural gaps in official records. Findings will focus equally on the missing data points as much as it does on the recorded information.

Workflow:
- APIs provided by city open data portals (e.g., NYC, Chicago, San Francisco, and Austin) have been accessed using `soda.py`, a Python library for the Socrata Open Data API. This allowed for structured and automated data retrieval.
- While searching through each open data portal, a wide range of keyword variations had to be used across the three subject categories (incarceration-related deaths, COVID-19 deaths, and drug-related deaths), in order to surface any relevant records, where available.
- To ensure consistency and prepare the datasets for analysis and visualization, `OpenRefine` was used as a key tool for cleaning and transforming the raw data retrieved from each city’s open data portal.
- Cleaned datasets were analyzed using Python libraries such as `pandas` for data manipulation and `plot.ly` for interactive visualizations.

Tools used:
-  `sodapy` python library
- `OpenRefine`
- `pandas` python library
- `plotly` python library

[View the Github repo](https://github.com/afran190/urban_mortality_data)

[View the live site](https://afran190.github.io/urban_mortality_data/)
